steps.title: Identify the logical gap the blank must fill
steps.content: The passage contrasts two generations: contemporary painters like White Hawk use motifs from indigenous beadwork and traditional arts, making their indigenous identity visible in the style itself. Scholder's generation instead aligned with Abstract Expressionism — a style dominated by European American artists — rather than traditional indigenous visual forms. Because their style is indistinguishable from European American abstract art, something other than style must determine whether such a painting is identified as Indigenous.

steps.title: Test each choice against that logic
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘place a greater emphasis on the artist's biography than on the aesthetic merit of the painting.’: The passage is about how identification happens, not about valuing biography over merit. Introducing "merit" as a factor adds something unsupported by the text. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘obscure the indigenous origins of certain motifs associated with Abstract Expressionism.’: The passage does not claim that Abstract Expressionism has indigenous origins — only that Scholder's generation adopted it instead of indigenous visual traditions. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘depend not on stylistic details but instead on an awareness of the artist's identity.’: Since Scholder's style is visually identical to European American Abstract Expressionism, viewers cannot use style as a signal. Identification must therefore depend on knowing who the artist is. This follows directly from the contrast the passage sets up. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘deny the extent to which cultural identity influences an artist's work.’: The passage does not argue that cultural identity has no influence; it argues only that the influence is not visible in the style. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
